expanded_description: Trigonometric functions of any angle extend trigonometry beyond acute angles in right triangles to any angle—positive, negative, greater than 360°, in any quadrant. This is fundamental to all trigonometric work—real-world applications rarely involve only acute angles. Wave motion, circular motion, AC circuits, and navigation all use angles in all quadrants. The key tools are reference angles (acute angle to x-axis), coterminal angles (angles differing by 360° or 2π), and the ASTC rule (which functions are positive in each quadrant). Together, these allow evaluating any trig function for any angle systematically. In Pre-calculus, mastering this is essential for solving trigonometric equations, graphing trigonometric functions, verifying trigonometric identities, and preparing for calculus. The process is systematic: reduce to 0°-360° using coterminal angles, find reference angle, get trig value for reference angle, apply sign from ASTC. This works for all six trig functions (sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, cot). Understanding this process builds confidence—you can evaluate trig functions for any angle, not just special angles. In applications, this enables analyzing wave motion at any phase, circular motion at any position, AC circuits at any time. This section teaches trig functions of any angle—systematic process, all six functions, degrees and radians, and applications.

core_concept.principle:
Trig Functions of Any Angle:
For any angle θ in standard position:
- sin θ = y-coordinate on unit circle
- cos θ = x-coordinate on unit circle
- tan θ = y/x (when x ≠ 0)
- csc θ = 1/y (when y ≠ 0)
- sec θ = 1/x (when x ≠ 0)
- cot θ = y/x (when y ≠ 0)

Evaluation Process:
1. Find coterminal angle between 0° and 360° (or 0 and 2π)
- Add/subtract 360° (or 2π) until in range
2. Determine which quadrant the angle is in
3. Find reference angle θ'
- QI: θ' = θ
- QII: θ' = 180° - θ (or π - θ)
- QIII: θ' = θ - 180° (or θ - π)
- QIV: θ' = 360° - θ (or 2π - θ)
4. Find trig value for reference angle θ'
5. Apply correct sign using ASTC rule
- QI: All positive
- QII: Sine positive
- QIII: Tangent positive
- QIV: Cosine positive

Coterminal Angles:
θ and θ + 360°n (or θ + 2πn) are coterminal
Coterminal angles have same trig values
core_concept.explanation: Think of evaluating trig functions for any angle as a systematic process—like a recipe. Step 1: reduce the angle to 0°-360° range using coterminal angles (add/subtract 360°). Step 2: figure out which quadrant you're in. Step 3: find the reference angle (acute angle to x-axis). Step 4: get the trig value for that reference angle (use special angles or calculator). Step 5: apply the correct sign based on which quadrant (ASTC rule). This works for ANY angle—positive, negative, huge angles. For example, sin(750°): 750° - 720° = 30° (coterminal), 30° is in QI, reference is 30°, sin(30°) = 1/2, QI is positive, so sin(750°) = 1/2. The beauty is it's systematic—same process for any angle. Coterminal angles are key—angles that differ by full rotations (360°) have same terminal side, same trig values. This is why trig functions are periodic—they repeat every 360°. Understanding this process builds confidence—you can evaluate trig for any angle. This section teaches the process—step-by-step for any angle, all six functions.

image.alt: Overview of trig functions of any angle showing evaluation process and ASTC rule
image.caption: Figure 7.5.1: Visual summary of evaluating trig functions for any angle: coterminal reduction, reference angle, ASTC sign rule, and evaluation process.

sections.introduction: Coterminal angles are angles that share the same terminal side—they differ by full rotations (360° or 2π radians). This means they have identical trigonometric function values. To evaluate trig functions for large angles or negative angles, we first reduce to a coterminal angle between 0° and 360° (or 0 and 2π radians). This simplifies evaluation significantly.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to coterminal angles and reduction
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.5.2: Introducing coterminal angles and reduction to 0°-360° range.

key_concept.definition:
Coterminal Angles:
Angles that share the same terminal side.
θ and θ + 360°n (or θ + 2πn) are coterminal for any integer n.

Finding Coterminal Angles:
In Degrees: Add or subtract 360° repeatedly
In Radians: Add or subtract 2π repeatedly

Reduction to Standard Range:
For positive angles > 360°: Subtract 360° until between 0° and 360°
For negative angles: Add 360° until between 0° and 360°

Why It Works:
Full rotations (360°) bring you back to same position
Same terminal side = same trig values

Examples:
390° = 30° + 360° → coterminal with 30°
-30° = 330° - 360° → coterminal with 330°
750° = 30° + 2(360°) → coterminal with 30°

Key Property:
All six trig functions have same values for coterminal angles
sin(θ) = sin(θ + 360°n) for any integer n
key_concept.context: Coterminal angles are fundamental to trigonometry—they explain why trig functions are periodic. In physics, this explains why wave motion repeats every 360°. In engineering, this explains why AC circuits repeat every cycle. In navigation, this explains why bearings repeat every 360°. The ability to reduce any angle to 0°-360° range is practical—you can evaluate trig functions for any angle using only values in this range. This is why trig tables and calculators only need values for 0°-360°. Understanding coterminal angles builds understanding of periodicity—why trig functions repeat. This section teaches coterminal angles—how to find them and use them for reduction.

image.alt: Coterminal angles diagram
image.caption: Figure 7.5.3: Diagram showing coterminal angles (30°, 390°, -330°) all sharing same terminal side.

image.alt: Example finding coterminal for large positive angles
image.caption: Figure 7.5.4: Reducing large positive angles to 0°-360° range.

worked_examples.problem:
Find a coterminal angle between 0° and 360° for:
a) 450°
b) 750°
c) 1000°

step_by_step.explanation: List all coterminal angles in standard range.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Large positive angles: subtract 360° repeatedly until between 0° and 360°.

worked_examples.title: Finding Coterminal Angles for Negative Angles

image.alt: Example finding coterminal for negative angles
image.caption: Figure 7.5.5: Reducing negative angles to 0°-360° range.

worked_examples.problem:
Find a coterminal angle between 0° and 360° for:
a) -30°
b) -90°
c) -200°

step_by_step.explanation: List all coterminal angles in standard range.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Negative angles: add 360° until between 0° and 360°.

worked_examples.title: Using Coterminal Angles to Evaluate Trig Functions

image.alt: Example using coterminal angles for trig evaluation
image.caption: Figure 7.5.6: Using coterminal angles to evaluate trigonometric functions.

step_by_step.explanation:
Find coterminal: -30° + 360° = 330°.
tan(-30°) = tan(330°).
330° is in QIV, tan negative, ref 30°.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Coterminal angles have same trig values; reduce to 0°-360°, evaluate using reference angles and ASTC.

image.alt: Coterminal angles summary
image.caption: Figure 7.5.7: Summary of coterminal angles and reduction to standard range.

sections.introduction: Reference angles are the acute angles between the terminal side of an angle and the x-axis. They are always between 0° and 90° (or 0 and π/2 radians). Reference angles allow us to find trigonometric values for any angle by relating them to acute angles in the first quadrant. The reference angle formula depends on which quadrant the angle is in.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to reference angles in all quadrants
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.5.8: Introducing reference angles in all four quadrants.

key_concept.definition:
Reference Angle Definition:
The acute angle (0° to 90° or 0 to π/2) between the terminal side and the x-axis.
Always positive and acute.
Denoted as θ' (theta prime).

Reference Angle Formulas by Quadrant:

Quadrant I (0° to 90° or 0 to π/2):
θ' = θ

Quadrant II (90° to 180° or π/2 to π):
θ' = 180° - θ (or π - θ)

Quadrant III (180° to 270° or π to 3π/2):
θ' = θ - 180° (or θ - π)

Quadrant IV (270° to 360° or 3π/2 to 2π):
θ' = 360° - θ (or 2π - θ)

For Negative Angles:
First find positive coterminal angle, then find reference angle.

Key Properties:
- Reference angle is always acute (0° < θ' < 90°)
- Reference angle is always positive
- Used to find trig values for any angle
- Same reference angle = same trig value magnitude (sign depends on quadrant)
key_concept.context: Reference angles are practical tools—they let you evaluate trig functions for any angle using only first quadrant values. In the days before calculators, trig tables only listed values for 0° to 90°—reference angles allowed finding values for any angle. Even with calculators, understanding reference angles builds understanding of trig function behavior. In physics, reference angles simplify wave calculations. In engineering, they simplify AC circuit analysis. In navigation, they simplify bearing calculations. Understanding reference angles reveals the symmetry of trig functions—why sine is positive in QI and QII, cosine in QI and QIV, etc. The ASTC rule (All Students Take Calculus) helps remember which functions are positive in each quadrant. This section teaches reference angles—how to find them in each quadrant and how to use them.

image.alt: Reference angles in all quadrants diagram
image.caption: Figure 7.5.9: Diagram showing reference angles in all four quadrants with formulas for each quadrant.

worked_examples.title: Finding Reference Angles in Degrees

image.alt: Example finding reference angles in degrees in all quadrants
image.caption: Figure 7.5.10: Finding reference angles for angles in all four quadrants.

step_by_step.explanation: 150° is in QII. Use θ' = 180° - θ.
210° is in QIII. Use θ' = θ - 180°.
300° is in QIV. Use θ' = 360° - θ.

First find positive coterminal: -60° + 360° = 300°.
300° is in QIV.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reference angles: QI: θ' = θ, QII: θ' = 180° - θ, QIII: θ' = θ - 180°, QIV: θ' = 360° - θ.

worked_examples.title: Finding Reference Angles in Radians

image.alt: Example finding reference angles in radians in all quadrants
image.caption: Figure 7.5.11: Finding reference angles for angles in radians in all four quadrants.

step_by_step.explanation: 2π/3 is in QII. Use θ' = π - θ.
5π/4 is in QIII. Use θ' = θ - π.
7π/6 is in QIII. Use θ' = θ - π.
11π/6 is in QIV. Use θ' = 2π - θ.
Check all are between 0 and π/2.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reference angles in radians: QI: θ' = θ, QII: θ' = π - θ, QIII: θ' = θ - π, QIV: θ' = 2π - θ.

worked_examples.title: Using Reference Angles to Find Trig Values

image.alt: Example using reference angles for trig values in all quadrants
image.caption: Figure 7.5.12: Using reference angles to find trigonometric function values in all quadrants.

worked_examples.problem:
Use reference angles and ASTC to find:
a) sin(150°)
b) cos(210°)
c) tan(300°)

step_by_step.explanation:
150° is in QII. Reference angle: 180° - 150° = 30°.
In QII, sine is positive (ASTC: Sine positive in QII).

210° is in QIII. Reference angle: 210° - 180° = 30°.
In QIII, cosine is negative (ASTC: Tangent positive in QIII).

300° is in QIV. Reference angle: 360° - 300° = 60°.
In QIV, tangent is negative (ASTC: Cosine positive in QIV).
Check signs using All Students Take Calculus.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reference angles + ASTC rule: find reference angle, get trig value for reference angle, apply sign based on quadrant.

image.alt: Reference angles summary
image.caption: Figure 7.5.13: Summary of reference angles, how to find them, and how to use them for trig values.

sections.introduction: The ASTC rule (All Students Take Calculus) is a mnemonic for remembering which trigonometric functions are positive in each quadrant. This rule, combined with reference angles, allows you to find trigonometric values for any angle. Understanding quadrant signs is essential for determining the correct sign when using reference angles.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to ASTC rule for all quadrants
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.5.14: Introducing ASTC rule for quadrant signs in all four quadrants.

key_concept.definition:
ASTC Rule:
Mnemonic for which trig functions are positive in each quadrant:

Quadrant I (0° to 90°): A - All functions positive
(sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, cot all positive)

Quadrant II (90° to 180°): S - Sine positive
(sin and csc positive; cos, sec, tan, cot negative)

Quadrant III (180° to 270°): T - Tangent positive
(tan and cot positive; sin, csc, cos, sec negative)

Quadrant IV (270° to 360°): C - Cosine positive
(cos and sec positive; sin, csc, tan, cot negative)

Mnemonic Phrases:
- All Students Take Calculus
- All Silver Tea Cups
- All Students Take Chemistry

Using ASTC with Reference Angles:
1. Find reference angle θ'
2. Find trig value for θ' (always positive, θ' is acute)
3. Determine quadrant of original angle
4. Apply ASTC rule to determine sign
5. Combine value and sign for final answer

Why It Works:
Based on x and y coordinates on unit circle:
- sin θ = y (positive in QI, QII)
- cos θ = x (positive in QI, QIV)
- tan θ = y/x (positive when x,y same sign: QI, QIII)
key_concept.context: ASTC rule is practical—it lets you determine signs without memorizing all quadrant rules. In physics, this determines direction of vector components. In engineering, this determines phase relationships. In navigation, this determines bearing quadrants. Understanding ASTC builds understanding of trig function behavior in different quadrants. The mnemonic makes it easy to remember—All Students Take Calculus is most common. The rule works because of unit circle geometry—sine is y-coordinate, cosine is x-coordinate, tangent is y/x. This section teaches ASTC rule—what it means, how to use it, and why it works.

image.alt: ASTC rule diagram for all quadrants
image.caption: Figure 7.5.15: Diagram showing ASTC rule with all four quadrants and which functions are positive in each quadrant.

worked_examples.title: Using ASTC Rule for All Six Trig Functions

image.alt: Example using ASTC for all six trig functions
image.caption: Figure 7.5.16: Using ASTC rule to find all six trigonometric function values.

worked_examples.problem: Evaluate all six trig functions for θ = 210°.

step_by_step.explanation: QIII: Tangent positive (T in ASTC), others negative.
Use reference angle values with correct signs.
tan = sin/cos or use reference angle.

worked_examples.concept_applied: All six trig functions: find reference angle, get sin/cos, apply ASTC signs, find reciprocals for csc/sec/cot.

worked_examples.title: Using ASTC Rule in Radians

image.alt: Example using ASTC rule in radians
image.caption: Figure 7.5.17: Using ASTC rule to find trigonometric function values in radians.

step_by_step.explanation:
5π/6 is in QII. Reference: π - 5π/6 = π/6.
In QII, sine is positive (ASTC: Sine positive in QII).

5π/4 is in QIII. Reference: 5π/4 - π = π/4.
In QIII, cosine is negative (ASTC: Tangent positive in QIII).

5π/3 is in QIV. Reference: 2π - 5π/3 = π/3.
In QIV, tangent is negative (ASTC: Cosine positive in QIV).
Check all angles are in correct quadrants.
step_by_step.calculation:
5π/6 in QII ✓
5π/4 in QIII ✓
5π/3 in QIV ✓

worked_examples.concept_applied: ASTC rule works in radians too; find reference angle, get value, apply sign based on quadrant.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Trig Functions for Negative Angles

image.alt: Example evaluating trig for negative angles
image.caption: Figure 7.5.18: Using ASTC rule to evaluate trigonometric functions for negative angles.

step_by_step.explanation: Find coterminal: -30° + 360° = 330°. In QIV, sine negative.
Find coterminal: -45° + 360° = 315°. In QIV, cosine positive.
Find coterminal: -60° + 360° = 300°. In QIV, tangent negative.

sin is odd: sin(-θ) = -sin(θ).
cos is even: cos(-θ) = cos(θ).
tan is odd: tan(-θ) = -tan(θ).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Negative angles: find coterminal positive angle OR use odd/even properties (sin, tan odd; cos even).

image.alt: ASTC rule summary
image.caption: Figure 7.5.19: Summary of ASTC rule and how to use it for all trig functions.

sections.introduction: The six trigonometric functions (sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, cot) are all related. Once you know sin and cos for an angle, you can find the other four using reciprocal and ratio relationships. This section teaches how to evaluate all six trig functions for any angle using reference angles and ASTC.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to all six trig functions
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.5.20: Introducing all six trigonometric functions and their relationships.

key_concept.definition:
Six Trigonometric Functions:

Primary Functions:
sin θ = y-coordinate on unit circle
cos θ = x-coordinate on unit circle
tan θ = y/x = sin θ/cos θ (when cos θ ≠ 0)

Reciprocal Functions:
csc θ = 1/sin θ (when sin θ ≠ 0)
sec θ = 1/cos θ (when cos θ ≠ 0)
cot θ = 1/tan θ = x/y (when tan θ ≠ 0)

Evaluation Process for All Six:
1. Find reference angle θ'
2. Find sin θ' and cos θ' (use special angles or calculator)
3. Apply ASTC signs to sin and cos
4. Find tan = sin/cos with correct sign
5. Find reciprocals: csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan
6. Apply same signs to reciprocals

Undefined Values:
tan, sec undefined when cos = 0 (90°, 270°)
cot, csc undefined when sin = 0 (0°, 180°)

Special Angle Values:
Memorize sin, cos for 30°, 45°, 60°
Derive tan, csc, sec, cot from sin and cos
key_concept.context: All six trig functions are interconnected—knowing sin and cos gives you all six. This is powerful—you only need to memorize sin and cos for special angles, then derive the rest. In calculus, all six functions appear—tan, sec, csc, cot have their own derivatives and integrals. In physics, all six appear in different contexts—tan for slopes, sec for certain projections, etc. In engineering, all six appear in AC circuit analysis, wave mechanics, etc. Understanding the relationships builds efficiency—you don't need to memorize all six separately. The reciprocal relationships (csc = 1/sin, etc.) are easy to remember. The ratio relationships (tan = sin/cos, etc.) connect the functions. This section teaches all six trig functions—how to evaluate all six for any angle.

image.alt: All six trig functions relationships diagram
image.caption: Figure 7.5.21: Diagram showing relationships between all six trigonometric functions.

image.alt: Example evaluating all six trig for special angle
image.caption: Figure 7.5.22: Evaluating all six trigonometric functions for a special angle.

worked_examples.problem: Evaluate all six trig functions for θ = 60°.
worked_examples.concept_applied: All six trig functions: find sin/cos first, then derive tan, csc, sec, cot using relationships.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating All Six Trig Functions for Any Angle

image.alt: Example evaluating all six trig for any angle
image.caption: Figure 7.5.23: Evaluating all six trigonometric functions for any angle using reference angles.

worked_examples.problem: Evaluate all six trig functions for θ = 330°.

step_by_step.explanation: QIV: Cosine positive (C in ASTC), others negative.
Use reference angle values with correct signs.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Any angle: find reference angle, get sin/cos, apply ASTC signs, find all six using relationships.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Trig Functions for Angles in Radians

image.alt: Example evaluating all six trig in radians
image.caption: Figure 7.5.24: Evaluating all six trigonometric functions for angles in radians.

worked_examples.problem: Evaluate all six trig functions for θ = 4π/3.

step_by_step.explanation: QIII: Tangent positive (T in ASTC), others negative.
Use reference angle values with correct signs.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Radians work same as degrees: find reference angle, get sin/cos, apply ASTC signs, find all six.

image.alt: All six trig functions summary
image.caption: Figure 7.5.25: Summary of evaluating all six trigonometric functions.

sections.section_title: 7.5.5 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Evaluating trig functions for any angle introduces specific pitfalls related to quadrant identification, reference angle formulas, sign determination, and special angle values. Recognizing common mistakes helps students develop careful habits and produce accurate results. Understanding where errors typically occur enables self-correction and better problem-solving.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to trig any angle errors
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.5.26: Overview of common errors when evaluating trig functions for any angle.

key_concept.definition:
Common Errors:

1. Wrong Quadrant Identification:
- Misidentifying which quadrant angle is in
- Confusing angle ranges for quadrants
- Not converting to 0°-360° range first

2. Wrong Reference Angle Formula:
- Using wrong formula for quadrant
- Mixing up QII and QIII formulas
- Forgetting reference angle is always acute

3. Wrong Sign from ASTC:
- Using wrong ASTC letter for quadrant
- Forgetting which functions positive in each quadrant
- Applying wrong sign to trig value

4. Special Angle Value Errors:
- Mixing up sin and cos values
- Wrong values for 30°, 45°, 60°
- Forgetting √2/2, √3/2 patterns

5. Radian/Degree Confusion:
- Mixing radians and degrees in same problem
- Using degree values with radian angles
- Wrong conversion between radians and degrees

Error Prevention:
- Draw angle in standard position to verify quadrant
- Memorize reference angle formulas for each quadrant
- Use ASTC mnemonic consistently
- Memorize special angle pattern (sin: 1-2-3, cos: 3-2-1)
- Keep track of units (degrees vs. radians)
- Verify answer makes sense (check quadrant signs)
key_concept.context: Trig evaluation errors are common—quadrant confusion leads to wrong signs. Reference angle formula errors give wrong reference angles. ASTC errors give wrong signs for trig functions. Special angle value errors (mixing up sin/cos values) give wrong values. Radian/degree confusion gives completely wrong answers. Understanding these errors develops trig literacy—knowing which quadrant, which formula, which sign, which values. In calculus, errors mean wrong trig evaluations. In physics, errors mean wrong wave/circular motion calculations. In engineering, errors mean wrong AC circuit analysis. This section teaches error awareness—critical for accurate trig work.

image.alt: Trig any angle errors diagram
image.caption: Figure 7.5.27: Diagram showing common errors when evaluating trig functions for any angle and how to avoid them.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Quadrant Identification Error

image.alt: Example correcting quadrant identification error
image.caption: Figure 7.5.28: Correcting errors in quadrant identification for trig evaluation.

worked_examples.problem: A student evaluated sin(210°) as +1/2. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation:
In QIII, only Tangent is positive (T in ASTC).
Sine is negative in QIII.

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: wrong sign from ASTC; Correct: sin(210°) = -1/2
worked_examples.concept_applied: ASTC rule: determine quadrant, check which functions positive, apply correct sign to reference angle value.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Reference Angle Formula Error

image.alt: Example correcting reference angle formula error
image.caption: Figure 7.5.29: Correcting errors in reference angle formulas.

worked_examples.problem: A student found reference angle for 300° as 300° - 180° = 120°. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: QIV uses θ' = 360° - θ, not θ - 180°.
step_by_step.calculation:
Student used QIII formula (θ - 180°)
Should use QIV formula (360° - θ)

step_by_step.explanation: Reference angle should be between 0° and 90°.

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used wrong formula (QIII instead of QIV); Correct: reference angle = 60°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reference angle formulas: QI: θ, QII: 180°-θ, QIII: θ-180°, QIV: 360°-θ; always acute.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Special Angle Value Error

image.alt: Example correcting special angle value error
image.caption: Figure 7.5.30: Correcting errors in special angle trigonometric values.

worked_examples.problem: A student evaluated cos(60°) as √3/2. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation: Student confused sin and cos values

step_by_step.explanation: Use unit circle or special triangle.

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: confused sin and cos values; Correct: cos(60°) = 1/2
worked_examples.concept_applied: Special angles: cos 60° = 1/2, sin 60° = √3/2; remember pattern (cos: 3-2-1 under √/2).

image.alt: Trig any angle errors summary
image.caption: Figure 7.5.31: Summary of common errors when evaluating trig functions for any angle.

sections.section_title: 7.5.6 Comprehensive Practice with Trig Functions of Any Angle
sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to trig functions of any angle—coterminal angles, reference angles, ASTC rule, evaluating all six trig functions, and error avoidance—to demonstrate mastery. These problems integrate multiple concepts and prepare students for trigonometric equations, identities, and graphing.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.alt: Comprehensive practice problems

1) Coterminal of 450°?

7) cos(210°) = ?

10) cos(-45°) = ?
11) tan(750°) = ?
12) sin(5π/6) = ?
13) cos(5π/4) = ?
14) tan(5π/3) = ?
15) All six trig for 60°?
16) All six trig for 330°?
17) All six trig for 4π/3?
18) What quadrant is 200° in?

20) Why use reference angles?

worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of trig functions of any angle—coterminal, reference angles, ASTC, all six functions.

image.alt: Complete practice solutions
image.caption: Figure 7.5.33: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: Trig functions of any angle: use coterminal angles to reduce to 0°-360°, find reference angle (acute angle to x-axis), apply ASTC rule for signs. Reference angles: QI: θ, QII: 180°-θ, QIII: θ-180°, QIV: 360°-θ. ASTC: QI All +, QII Sine +, QIII Tangent +, QIV Cosine +. All six trig functions: sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, cot; find sin/cos first, derive others using relationships (tan=sin/cos, csc=1/sin, etc.). Special angles: 30°, 45°, 60° have exact values (memorize sin/cos, derive others). Works for degrees and radians. Common errors: wrong quadrant, wrong reference angle formula, wrong ASTC sign, mixing sin/cos values, radian/degree confusion. Coterminal angles have same trig values (differ by 360° or 2π). Reference angles allow evaluating trig for any angle using first quadrant values. Essential for trigonometric equations, identities, graphing, and applications in physics, engineering, and calculus.

review_questions_image.alt: Comprehensive summary: trig functions of any angle, coterminal angles, reference angles, ASTC rule, all six trig functions, special angles, common errors
review_questions_image.caption: Figure 7.5.34: Complete reference for trigonometric functions of any angle showing coterminal angle reduction, reference angle formulas, ASTC rule, all six trig functions, and evaluation process.
